# Scorched-Earth Erdős Sweep — Turn 121

This is turn 121. Turns 1 through 120 are consolidated in `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` (attached). Re-read it before answering.
Also re-read `mega_prompt.md` and `problem_statement.md`.

CRITICAL — use ONLY the files attached to THIS message. Do NOT open, import, retrieve, or cite any file from your File Library / previously uploaded files, even one named similarly (e.g. another `turns_history…md`). Other unrelated problems use similarly named history files in the same account, and importing one will silently switch you onto the wrong problem. If the attached `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` appears missing, do NOT continue from partial context. Reply only with `MISSING_REQUIRED_ATTACHMENT` and name the missing file(s); never substitute a library file.

If this turn drifts into hammering one specific case or sub-instance, step back and ask whether the case is revealing a structural pattern that would close many cases at once. Prefer arguments whose conclusion is a uniform statement.

End this response with ESTABLISHED / GAP / NEXT + tag.
Continue with Turn 121.

## Turn 121 — the residual determinant quotient is an actual central pushout

The candidate remains unchanged:

```text
sigma,tau,x0,x1

sigma2 = sigma^40491355905
u0 = (x0*tau)^40491355905
u1 = (x1*tau)^40491355905
d0 = u0*x0^-1
z0 = x0^sigma2
c0 = d0^-1*z0^-1*d0*z0

x0^2*x1^4*u1^-1*(x1^sigma)*c0
```

The tame relation is implicit. Proof-level exponents mean the genuine profinite idempotent \(\omega _2\); \(40491355905\) is its required verifier residue. 

Turn 120 canonically decomposed the full \(R\)-obstruction into a uniformly distributed radical-edge quotient and a residual obstruction whose dual is \(D_{\det}=\ker E\).  The new point is that this residual quotient is represented by an **actual quotient of the finite target**, not merely by an abstract quotient of an obstruction space.

Retain
\[
R=\Phi(K),\qquad M=K/R,\qquad
T_0=\operatorname{rad}_{\mathbf F_2[C]}M,\qquad V=M/T_0,
\]
and
\[
B=Y/R,\qquad C=Y/K.
\]

Put
\[
D_R=(R^\vee)^B,\qquad
E:D_R\longrightarrow H^1(C,T_0^\vee),\qquad
D_{\det}=\ker E.
\]

### 1. A target-level radical-edge subgroup

Let
\[
R_{\mathrm{tr}}:=R/[B,R].
\]
This is the maximal trivial \(B\)-quotient of \(R\), and
\[
D_R=(R^\vee)^B\cong R_{\mathrm{tr}}^\vee.
\tag{121.1}
\]

Dualizing \(E\) gives a target-defined map
\[
E^\vee:H^1(C,T_0^\vee)^\vee\longrightarrow R_{\mathrm{tr}}.
\]

Define
\[
R_{\mathrm{edge}}:=\operatorname{im}E^\vee,\qquad
R_{\det}:=R_{\mathrm{tr}}/R_{\mathrm{edge}}.
\tag{121.2}
\]

Then annihilator duality gives
\[
R_{\det}^\vee
=
\{\lambda\in R_{\mathrm{tr}}^\vee:\lambda(R_{\mathrm{edge}})=0\}
=
\ker E
=
D_{\det}.
\tag{121.3}
\]

Let \(N_{\mathrm{edge}}\le R\) be the inverse image of \(R_{\mathrm{edge}}\) under
\[
R\longrightarrow R_{\mathrm{tr}}.
\]
Thus
\[
R/N_{\mathrm{edge}}\cong R_{\det}.
\]

Since \(R_{\mathrm{tr}}\) has trivial \(B\)-action, \(N_{\mathrm{edge}}\triangleleft Y\), and
\[
Y_{\det}:=Y/N_{\mathrm{edge}}
\]
fits into a canonical central extension
\[
1\longrightarrow R_{\det}
\longrightarrow Y_{\det}
\xrightarrow{p_{\det}} B
\longrightarrow 1.
\tag{121.4}
\]

So the residual determinant obstruction has a genuine finite-group representative.

### 2. The Turn-120 variation map is exactly \(E^\vee\)

For a fixed exact lower map \(\rho:\Gamma\twoheadrightarrow C\), Proposition 3 gives the natural identification
\[
\operatorname{Ob}_\rho(R)^\vee\cong D_R,
\]
hence
\[
\operatorname{Ob}_\rho(R)\cong D_R^\vee\cong R_{\mathrm{tr}}.
\tag{121.5}
\]

Under this identification, the Turn-120 variation map
\[
\delta_\rho:H^1_\rho(T_0)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Ob}_\rho(R)
\]
factors as
\[
H^1_\rho(T_0)
\xrightarrow{\mathrm{PD}_\rho}
H^1_\rho(T_0^\vee)^\vee
\xrightarrow{(\rho^*)^\vee}
H^1(C,T_0^\vee)^\vee
\xrightarrow{E^\vee}
R_{\mathrm{tr}}.
\tag{121.6}
\]

Here \(\mathrm{PD}_\rho\) is the perfect common-word pairing. The pullback
\[
\rho^*:H^1(C,T_0^\vee)\hookrightarrow H^1_\rho(T_0^\vee)
\]
is injective, so its dual is surjective. Consequently,
\[
\operatorname{im}\delta_\rho
=
\operatorname{im}E^\vee
=
R_{\mathrm{edge}}.
\tag{121.7}
\]

Thus Turn 120’s abstract space \(O_{\mathrm{rad},\rho}\) is canonically the fixed target quotient \(R_{\mathrm{edge}}\), independently of the source and of \(\rho\).

### 3. \(Y_{\det}\) represents the residual obstruction

Let \(f:\Gamma\to B\) be an exact boundary-framed map. Under (121.5), let
\[
o_R(f)\in R_{\mathrm{tr}}
\]
be its full \(R\)-obstruction.

Its image
\[
\bar o_R(f)\in R_{\det}
\]
is precisely the obstruction to lifting \(f\) through \(Y_{\det}\to B\).

Indeed, every
\[
\lambda\in R_{\det}^\vee=D_{\det}
\]
defines the same scalar pushout whether it is applied first to \(Y\) or to \(Y_{\det}\). Hence \(\lambda(\bar o_R(f))\) is the scalar obstruction associated with \(\lambda\). Since the characters in \(R_{\det}^\vee\) separate points, the two residual obstructions coincide.

Turn 120’s coset uniformity therefore becomes the pointwise target-level identity
\[
\#\{f:o_R(f)=0\}
=
\frac{1}{|R_{\mathrm{edge}}|}
\#\{f:f\text{ lifts through }Y_{\det}\}.
\tag{121.8}
\]

This is stronger conceptually than restricted Fourier support: first quotient the target by \(N_{\mathrm{edge}}\), then divide by the uniform edge multiplicity.

### 4. A higher-rank central-cover transform

Let \(A\) be any elementary abelian central kernel contained in the boundary kernel, and let
\[
p:Z\twoheadrightarrow B
\]
be the corresponding central extension.

For an exact \(B\)-map whose obstruction vanishes, all lifts form a torsor under
\[
\operatorname{Hom}(\Pi_{\mathrm{el}},A),
\]
of size
\[
|\operatorname{Hom}(\Pi_{\mathrm{el}},A)|=|A|^3.
\]

Partitioning these lifts by their exact images gives the higher-rank version of Proposition 6:
\[
|A|^3\,u_\Gamma(p,B)
=
\sum_{\substack{J\le Z\\p(J)=B}}
e_\Gamma(J),
\tag{121.9}
\]
where \(u_\Gamma(p,B)\) counts exact \(B\)-maps whose pullback of \(p\) splits.

Apply this with \(Z=Y_{\det}\) and \(A=R_{\det}\).

If
\[
N_{\mathrm{edge}}\ne1,
\]
then every subgroup \(J\le Y_{\det}\) occurring in (121.9) has marked kernel bounded by
\[
|J\cap(L_Y/N_{\mathrm{edge}})|
\le
|L_Y/N_{\mathrm{edge}}|
<
|L_Y|.
\tag{121.10}
\]
All terms on the right of (121.9) are therefore already matched by the global strong induction. Equation (121.8) then matches the original \(Y\)-obstruction count automatically.

Hence **all nontrivial target-level edge quotients descend to smaller marked targets without invoking determinant Gauss sums or the inner Fourier transform.**

### 5. The only non-descending case is genuinely central and zero-edge

Suppose
\[
N_{\mathrm{edge}}=1.
\]
Because \(N_{\mathrm{edge}}\) contains the inverse image of \(0\) under
\[
R\to R/[B,R],
\]
we obtain
\[
[B,R]=1.
\]
Also \(R_{\mathrm{edge}}=\operatorname{im}E^\vee=0\), so
\[
E=0.
\]

Thus, unless \(R=1\), the only central stage that cannot be discharged immediately by strict induction satisfies simultaneously
\[
R\le Z(Y),\qquad E=0,\qquad D_{\det}=D_R=R^\vee.
\tag{121.11}
\]

This is exactly the pure central, zero-radical-edge determinant situation. Only here are the inner \(D_0\)-Fourier accessibility formula, shearing, the Wall–Arf/Kummer Gauss sum, and the smaller \(\Delta_{\lambda,a}\)-covers genuinely needed.

The central stage therefore admits the uniform dichotomy
\[
\boxed{
\begin{array}{ll}
N_{\mathrm{edge}}\ne1:
&\text{quotient to }Y_{\det}\text{ and descend strictly by induction};\\[2mm]
N_{\mathrm{edge}}=1:
&R\text{ is central and }E=0,\text{ so apply the pure determinant machinery}.
\end{array}}
\tag{121.12}
\]

This replaces the abstract “quotient the obstruction space first” instruction by an actual quotient of the finite target. It also confines the expensive determinant analysis to the unique structural configuration in which no target-level descent is available.

**ESTABLISHED**: Realized Turn 120’s residual determinant obstruction as the obstruction of a canonical central target quotient \(Y_{\det}=Y/N_{\mathrm{edge}}\). Proved the pointwise identity (121.8), generalized the boundary-safe central-cover transform to arbitrary elementary central kernels, and obtained the descent-or-central dichotomy (121.12). Every nontrivial radical-edge quotient now closes by strict marked-kernel induction; determinant machinery is needed only when \(R\) is already central and \(E=0\). The candidate word remains unchanged.

**GAP**: The private 5402-group verifier remains unrun, and the six-proposition manuscript has not received an independent external line-by-line review. The new higher-rank central-transform lemma must be inserted formally into Proposition 4.

**NEXT**: Rewrite Proposition 4 around \(N_{\mathrm{edge}}\): use (121.8)–(121.10) to discharge the strict-descent branch, and retain the inner \(D_0\)-Fourier/determinant argument only in the central zero-edge branch (121.11). Then freeze the manuscript and submit the unchanged candidate block.

[BREAKTHROUGH]
